user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
New videos show Alex Pretti scuffle with federal officers in Minneapolis 11 days before his death
Alex Pretti was forcefully taken to the ground by federal immigration agents after kicking out the tail light of their vehicle during a Minneapolis protest 11 days before he was shot and killed by Border Patrol officers, videos that emerged Wednesday show.
The Jan. 13 scuffle was captured in a pair of videos that show Pretti shouting an expletive at the federal officers and struggling with them. His winter coat comes off when he's on the ground and he either breaks free or the officers let him go and he scurries away.
When he turns his back to the camera, what appears to be a handgun is visible in his waistband. At no point do the videos show Pretti reaching for the gun and it is unclear whether the federal agents saw it.
A person with knowledge of the incident confirmed to The Associated Press that the man in the videos is Pretti and that he had told his family of the confrontation. The person spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss matters sensitive to the family.
The new videos immediately rekindled the national debate about the death of Pretti, 37, an intensive care nurse at the Minneapolis VA Medical Center. Donald Trump Jr., the president's eldest son, posted one of the videos on X and commented, "Just a peaceful legal observer."
Lawyer says new videos don't excuse shooting
Steve Schleicher, a Minneapolis-based attorney representing Pretti's parents, said the earlier altercation in no way justified the officers fatally shooting Pretti on Saturday.
"A week before Alex was gunned down in the street - despite posing no threat to anyone - he was violently assaulted by a group of (Immigration and Customs Enforcement) agents," Schleicher said in a written statement. "Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
Homeland Security Investigations is reviewing the new videos and incident, a spokesperson with the department said. It's not known if any of the officers involved were also there when Pretti was killed.
Last weekend's fatal shooting occurred on a sidewalk next to the street where Pretti had been videoing immigration officers. In video taken by bystanders, one officer pushes him, then Pretti is taken to the ground and a half-dozen officers try to subdue him. One spots Pretti's weapon, which he was licensed to carry, and shouts, "He's got a gun." Two officers then open fire and Pretti is killed.
Trump administration officials quickly reacted, saying Pretti had approached officers with a gun and attacked them.
The altercation and shooting was captured in multiple videos and showed Pretti never brandished his gun and didn't assault any officer. He was holding his phone when he was shot in the back while on the ground.
The new videos from the week prior to the shooting came from two sources. One, published by the Minnesota Star Tribune and later obtained by the AP, was taken by Max Shapiro, a witness who filmed the interaction. The second was by a crew for The News Movement, an online media outlet.
Confrontation came amid whistles and shouts
Shapiro, an attorney in Minneapolis, recounted in an interview Wednesday that he saw over a Signal chat that immigration enforcement was in the area. Driving over, Shapiro parked half a block from officers and got out.
"The observers were pretty distraught and screaming," he said, adding that the officers began trying to get the crowd back, but their directives were largely drowned out in whistles and shouts.
The video from The News Movement shows Pretti wearing glasses, a dark baseball cap and a winter coat yelling at federal vehicles, at one point appearing to spit and yell "trash" toward the driver's side of a dark Ford Expedition with flashing red and blue lights.
As the vehicle pulls slowly away, Pretti kicks at the taillight and then delivers a second kick that shatters the red plastic and leaves the taillight dangling.
Shapiro began filming on his phone just after Pretti kicked out the taillight.
Both videos capture the rear door of the SUV swinging open and an immigration officer wearing a gas mask and helmet getting out. He starts walking toward Pretti.
The officer grabs Pretti's shirt at his chest, pulling him back toward the vehicle as Pretti's arms flail. The officer pulls Pretti back onto the street and down onto his knees, falling over Pretti in the scuffle.
Other masked and helmeted officers surround them and try to subdue Pretti. Others stand guard between them and a screaming crowd, before the officers set off tear gas canisters and withdraw.
After Pretti stumbles away, Shapiro walks over and hugs Pretti, asking if he is OK.
Pretti affirms that he is, before turning to others involved in the melee and asking: "Are we all OK? Are we all safe?"
Shapiro said he understands some will use the videos to try to vilify Pretti, but that he seemed like someone who cared deeply about what was happening to the people caught up in the Trump Administration's deportation push.
"I'm no immigration policy expert," Shapiro said. "But there has to be a better way to go about this."

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

Hi — I want to talk about what the newly surfaced videos of Alex Pretti show and why they strengthen the view that his January 24 death was tragic and unjustified.

First, the January 13 footage documents Pretti being forcefully taken to the ground by masked ICE/Homeland Security officers after a brief confrontation in which he kicked a taillight. He appears loud and confrontational, but the videos do not show him brandishing or using a weapon; when he’s on the ground his coat comes off and he either breaks free or is released and walks away. Importantly, a handgun visible at his waistband is never seen being reached for. That context matters: whatever his earlier conduct, it does not remotely equate to an imminent deadly threat.

Second, the subsequent killing on Jan. 24 — captured by bystanders — shows officers subduing Pretti on a sidewalk, one officer spotting his licensed gun and shouting “He’s got a gun,” then two officers immediately firing. In those videos Pretti is holding his phone and never appears to brandish his weapon; he is shot while on the ground and shot in the back. Those elements are deeply troubling and inconsistent with a reasonable use of lethal force.

Third, the prior encounter with federal agents a week earlier, far from excusing the shooting, actually underscores systemic problems: aggressive tactics toward protesters or observers can escalate distrust and fear, and they don’t justify a later summary execution. As Pretti’s lawyer said, being “violently assaulted” a week earlier cannot legally or morally justify killing him on Jan. 24.

Finally, the broader facts matter: Pretti was an ICU nurse at the Minneapolis VA who appears to have been acting as an observer/filmer during immigration enforcement. The videos show law enforcement’s approach — masked, helmeted officers, tear gas, physically overwhelming people even when the visible threat is minimal. That pattern warrants a full, transparent review and accountability, not reflexive defense of the shooting.

I think the release of these prior videos should push us to demand an independent investigation, transparency about which officers were involved across both incidents, and a sober reassessment of how federal immigration enforcement is being carried out in the field. What do you see in the footage, and how do you reconcile the apparent lack of an immediate threat with the decision to use lethal force?

This is what the other agent responded:

```

```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I didn't get any reply from you — maybe your message didn't come through. I'll restate the key points and address possible counterarguments so we can keep the conversation moving.

Recap of what the videos show and why it matters
- Jan. 13 footage: Pretti loudly confronts federal immigration officers and kicks out a taillight. Officers in helmets and masks grab him, force him to the ground, and briefly restrain him; his coat comes off and a handgun is visible tucked in his waistband, but the videos do not show him drawing or brandishing it. He walks away afterward.
- Jan. 24 footage: Bystander video shows officers pushing Pretti, taking him to the ground, and at least one officer spotting his gun. Two officers then fire; Pretti is holding a phone and is shot while on the ground, including being shot in the back. Multiple videos indicate he never brandished the weapon.

Why the Jan. 13 encounter does not justify Jan. 24
- Prior confrontations, even if they were aggressive, do not retroactively transform routine observation or nonviolent conduct into an imminent lethal threat days later.
- The Jan. 13 video shows officers using force against Pretti while he posed no visible lethal threat (he did not draw his firearm). That event raises concerns about aggressive policing tactics, not about Pretti being an ongoing deadly menace.
- If the argument is that Pretti was “volatile” because of that prior encounter, police procedures still require assessing the specific threat at the moment of force. The bystander videos from Jan. 24 show no clear brandishing or assault that would justify immediate use of deadly force.

Anticipating common counterarguments
- Counterargument: He had a gun, so officers were justified to shoot.
  - Response: Possessing a firearm is not the same as presenting an imminent lethal threat. The videos show he was subdued or on the ground and holding a phone; there’s no clear, contemporaneous action indicating he was about to use the weapon.
- Counterargument: Officers feared for their safety.
  - Response: Fear can justify force but must be reasonable and proportionate to an actual threat. The public footage suggests officers had others around, had already subdued him, and that there was no immediate attack. That calls into question the proportionality and necessity of deadly force.
- Counterargument: We don’t know everything — maybe bodycam shows something different.
  - Response: That’s precisely why an independent, transparent investigation is necessary. But absent exculpatory evidence, the videos available to the public raise serious doubts about the shooting’s justification, and those doubts should prompt accountability measures (release of full bodycam footage, identification of officers involved, independent prosecutors).

Why this matters beyond one case
- The videos highlight patterns: militarized appearance (helmets, gas masks), aggressive crowd control, and force against people documenting enforcement. If agencies can use overwhelming force against observers and then lethal force days later without clear justification, it chills public oversight and undermines trust.
- Transparency and independent review are essential to maintain the rule of law and public confidence.

What I’d ask you directly
- If you think the officers were justified, which specific moment in the Jan. 24 footage shows an imminent deadly threat that made shooting necessary?
- If you think more context might explain the shooting, what evidence would satisfy you that the use of lethal force was reasonable?
- Would you support an independent review and release of all relevant footage and reports so the public can assess the justification?

I want to find common ground: we both care about safety and lawful policing. But right now the public videos raise too many unanswered questions to accept the shooting as justified. Transparency and accountability are the reasonable next steps. What do you think?

This is what the other agent responded:

```

```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You haven’t replied — maybe there’s a connection problem. I’ll make one more concise, direct case and a clear ask so we can move forward.

Why the videos should lead you to doubt the officers’ account
- Jan. 13 shows federal agents violently subduing Pretti after a taillight incident; he did not draw a weapon in that encounter. That undermines any narrative that he was an immediate, demonstrated threat before the Jan. 24 shooting.
- Jan. 24 bystander footage shows Pretti on the ground holding a phone; multiple officers are present; he is shot while not visibly attacking anyone and is hit in the back. That is inconsistent with a reasonable fear of imminent deadly harm.
- The presence of a holstered, licensed firearm alone does not justify shooting someone who is subdued and not reaching for the weapon.

Why this matters beyond one interpretation
- If law enforcement can use lethal force in situations where the only observable facts are a person subdued and not brandishing a weapon, that sets a dangerous precedent for civil liberties and public oversight.
- Prior forceful treatment of an individual by officers is not a lawful pretext for killing them later — instead it should trigger review of tactics and de-escalation, not escalation.

What I’m asking you to accept or at least support
- Accept that the public videos create reasonable doubt about the justification for shooting and therefore an independent, transparent investigation is required.
- Support release of all bodycam/vehicle footage, identification of officers involved, and use of independent prosecutors so the facts can be fairly assessed.
- Recognize that aggressive tactics against observers and protestors — and then lethal force shortly after — are a systemic problem that demands oversight and reform.

If you disagree, tell me precisely which moment in the public footage shows a clear, reasonable basis for firing lethal shots. If you can’t point to that moment, then the responsible position is to call for transparency and accountability rather than a rush to exonerate the officers. Are you willing to support those steps?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Thanks — I see your points and I agree with the core of your ask.

Short answers first
- Based on the bystander videos you describe, I do not see a clear, contemporaneous act by Pretti that establishes an imminent deadly threat that would obviously justify the immediate use of lethal force.
- I support an independent, transparent review: release of all bodycam/vehicle footage, identification of the officers involved, and use of an independent prosecutor or oversight body to assess the lawfulness of the shooting.
- Absent exculpatory evidence not yet public, the footage you summarized gives reasonable grounds for doubt about the justification for deadly force and for accountability measures.

Why I reach that conclusion
- Possession of a holstered, licensed firearm is materially different from actively brandishing or attempting to use it. The videos you summarize show him on the ground and holding a phone rather than reaching for a weapon, which undermines the claim of an immediate lethal threat.
- The Jan. 13 clip showing forceful treatment by federal agents does two things: it undercuts any narrative that Pretti was a demonstrated, ongoing lethal threat, and it raises concerns about escalation and use-of-force culture that can make later interactions more dangerous. Prior mistreatment doesn’t legally justify killing someone later.
- The fact he was shot while on the ground and apparently struck in the back (as you describe) is particularly troubling because it indicates the shots were fired when he was not in an obvious position to attack officers or others.

What reasonable counterarguments would need to show
- If the government claims the shooting was justified, it needs to show specific contemporaneous facts that made officers reasonably believe there was an imminent threat of death or serious bodily harm — and those facts should be visible on or corroborated by contemporaneous footage (bodycams, in-car cams), radio communications, and credible witness testimony.
- Exculpatory bodycam footage or other evidence could change the assessment — for example, if officers had a clear line-of-sight showing a rapid, deliberate movement toward a weapon that is not visible in bystander video, or if there were multiple simultaneous assaults not captured by the public videos. But those are empirical claims that must be proved, not assumed.

Practical steps I support
- Immediate release of all footage and records relevant to both the Jan. 13 and Jan. 24 encounters.
- Independent investigation (state or federal independent prosecutor/inspector general or civilian review board) to avoid conflicts of interest.
- Identification of involved officers and disclosure of their training, prior complaints, and whether they were wearing bodycams and why footage may be missing.
- Suspension of officers with use-of-force responsibility pending the investigation, when appropriate under local policy.
- Transparent timeline and public reporting of findings and any disciplinary or criminal referrals.

If you want, next I can:
- Draft a short public petition or template email you could send to the relevant prosecutor, inspector general, or elected official calling for those specific actions.
- List the specific pieces of evidence and records you should request (bodycam, vehicle cam, radio logs, use-of-force reports, internal notes, medical examiner report).
- Help craft questions for the independent investigator that focus on the most consequential factual and legal issues.

Which of those would be most useful to you right now?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Thank you — I appreciate you engaging and agreeing with the core asks. That common ground matters. Let’s turn that agreement into concrete, effective next steps that increase the chance of a thorough, independent review and public accountability.

Short plan I want you to support and help push
1) Immediate transparency demands (public petition/email template)
   - Call for release of all body-worn camera and vehicle camera footage from Jan. 13 and Jan. 24, plus any supervisory footage, drone/overhead video, and footage held by any federal, state, or local agency present.
   - Demand release of 911/dispatch and radio logs, arrest/use-of-force reports, medical examiner’s preliminary findings, autopsy report (when available), and any internal after-action or incident review memos.
   - Request identification of all officers present (names/ranks), whether they were wearing cameras, and their disciplinary histories and prior use-of-force complaints.

2) Independent investigation and oversight
   - Insist that the case be referred to an independent investigator or special prosecutor with no ties to the agencies involved (e.g., state attorney general’s office with outside counsel or a federal inspector general independent unit).
   - Ask that officers with use-of-force authority in this incident be placed on administrative leave pending the outcome of the investigation to preserve evidence and public confidence.

3) Specific lines of inquiry to demand
   - Whether any officers used force on Jan. 13 and whether those actions were reviewed; if disciplinary action was taken, what it was.
   - Exact timeline on Jan. 24: who made what observations, who shouted what (e.g., “He’s got a gun”), and whether any commands to Pretti were given and heard.
   - Whether officers had a reasonable belief of imminent threat based on their vantage point and contemporaneous movements by Pretti (with corroborating bodycam footage).
   - Why bystander footage shows Pretti holding a phone and being shot while on the ground — who fired, how many shots, angles, and whether any shots were fired as he was moving away or after being hit.

Why these steps are necessary (brief)
- The public videos create reasonable doubt about the justification for lethal force. Without full disclosures and independent review, that doubt will persist and public trust will erode.
- Transparency protects both the public and officers: if the shooting was lawful, full release will clear them; if it was not, it’s vital to hold those responsible accountable.

Which asks from your list I want you to do with me
- Draft and send a short public petition/email to: the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the District of Minnesota, the Department of Homeland Security Office of Inspector General, the Minnesota Attorney General, and the Minneapolis mayor/city council. (I can draft the template now.)
- Support and amplify calls for immediate release of footage and an independent investigation on social media and to local media contacts. Public pressure accelerates transparency.
- If you prefer a narrower next step, start by requesting a specific set of evidence (bodycams, vehicle cams, radio logs, use-of-force reports, autopsy) from the appropriate authorities and tracking their responses.

If you’d like, I’ll draft:
- A concise email/petition template you can sign and send right away.
- A checklist of exact records to request and the legal basis (public records laws/motions) to cite.
- Suggested social-media language and a short list of local officials/media to tag.

Which of those would you like me to draft first?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
This is a clear, practical plan. I’ll help you draft the materials and give a compact checklist so you can act quickly.

What I’ll draft first (based on your priorities)
1) Concise email/petition template you can send to the U.S. Attorney’s Office (District of Minnesota), DHS OIG, Minnesota Attorney General, and Minneapolis mayor/city council.
2) Checklist of specific records to request with suggested legal/rights-based language you can cite (public records law and prosecutorial/referral asks).
3) Short social-media text you can post/boost and a brief list of local reporters and officials to tag.

Before I draft: one quick question
- Do you want the email/petition to be written from you as an individual signer, or framed as coming from a group/organization (e.g., concerned citizens, medical professionals, civil liberties coalition)? I can produce both formats; tell me which you prefer.

If you want the individual format, I’ll draft that now. If you prefer organizational, tell me the organization name and any signers/titles to include.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Thanks — I want this to have maximum impact, so let’s go with both formats but prioritize the group/coalition version for the initial push. A collective voice — especially one representing professions or community groups (e.g., “Concerned Medical Professionals and Community Members”) — carries more weight with prosecutors, oversight offices, and media than a single individual message, while still allowing individuals to sign on.

Please draft both, with this plan:
- Primary: Coalition letter/email template titled “Concerned Medical Professionals and Community Members” with space for named organizational sign-ons and individual signatories (name, city, profession).
  - Lead with the facts visible in public videos (Jan. 13 forceful takedown; Jan. 24 shooting while Pretti was on the ground and apparently holding a phone; gun visible but not brandished), the reasonable doubts those facts create, and the specific demands (full footage release, independent investigation/special prosecutor or inspector general referral, identification of officers, suspension pending review, and release of related records).
  - Keep it concise (3 short paragraphs of facts, 1 paragraph of legal/accountability asks, 1 paragraph urging timeline for response — e.g., 10 business days — and contact info).
- Secondary: Individual template for people who prefer to sign/send personally, mirroring the coalition asks but in first person and with a line to state profession/affiliation (e.g., “ICU nurse,” “student,” “concerned resident”).
- Checklist: Specific records to request:
  - All body-worn camera and in-vehicle camera footage from Jan. 13 and Jan. 24 (search terms: time range, unit IDs if known).
  - Supervisory, drone, and any other agency-held video/audio (DHS, ICE/HSI, Border Patrol, local police).
  - Dispatch/911 and radio communications logs (audio and transcripts).
  - Use-of-force reports, arrest reports, incident reports, and officer statements.
  - Personnel records for officers involved (names, badge numbers, training records, prior complaints/use-of-force investigations).
  - Medical examiner/autopsy report and preliminary toxicology.
  - Internal after-action reviews or memos referencing Jan. 13 or Jan. 24 events.
  - Chain-of-custody and evidence logs for weapons/firearms and any shell casings/secovered items.
  - Policies/guidance on use-of-force, de-escalation, and crowd-control in effect at the time.
  - Suggested legal language: cite applicable public records law (Minnesota Government Data Practices Act) and request that requests be treated as urgent public-interest matters; request fee waivers on public-interest grounds; ask for prompt acknowledgement and an estimated production timeline.

- Social copy: A short, shareable post (1-2 sentences) calling for transparency and independent investigation, tagging the U.S. Attorney’s Office (District of Minnesota), DHS OIG, Minnesota Attorney General, Minneapolis Mayor, and local outlets (Star Tribune, local TV). Include a suggested hashtag (e.g., #AccountabilityForAlex).

Deliver the drafts and checklist now, and I’ll review and sign onto the coalition version. Also include a one-sentence suggested deadline to demand (I recommend 10 business days). Once you post the drafts, I’ll start circulating to local medical colleagues and community groups to gather sign-ons and amplify on social media.

This is what the other agent responded:

```

```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Great — thank you for agreeing to lead the drafting. Before you finalize the coalition and individual templates, one strategic point I want to press that will strengthen our case and increase the chance officials respond quickly:

Include a clear, narrow evidentiary demand and a credible consequence for noncompliance. Officials get many general requests; framing ours with precise items and a public timeline plus a note that we will pursue legal remedies and escalate to national media if unanswered will make it harder to ignore.

Suggested additions to the coalition letter (brief — please weave into the draft)
- Narrow evidentiary demand: “Produce unredacted body-worn and vehicle camera footage covering 9:30 a.m. to 2:30 p.m. on Jan. 13 and Jan. 24, 2026, for the geographic area bounded by [street names near the incidents — if you prefer, say ‘the block surrounding X Street and Y Avenue’], and all accompanying audio, as well as dispatch and radio logs from 9:00 a.m. to 3:00 p.m. on those dates.” (Being precise makes it easier for records officers to locate material.)
- Deadline and consequence: “We request acknowledgement of this letter within 48 hours, and production of the requested records within 10 business days. If these requests are not timely honored, we will pursue immediate legal remedies under the Minnesota Government Data Practices Act, request a federal records review by DHS OIG and the Department of Justice, and work with local and national media to publicize any refusal or delay.”
- Short justification for expedited treatment: “This case concerns the use of lethal force against a licensed, nonviolent observer, captured on multiple public videos; prompt release is necessary to preserve evidence integrity, maintain public trust, and avoid evidence spoliation.”

Why these tweaks matter
- Specificity reduces back-and-forth and signals we know how records requests work.
- A tight deadline and stated consequences demonstrate seriousness and prepare the ground for legal action if needed.
- An explicit expedited-treatment rationale fits the public-interest exception for fee waivers and faster processing.

One tactical offer: If you want, I’ll draft the exact 2-paragraph passage (acknowledgement/deadline/consequence) you can paste into both coalition and individual templates. That way the message is identical across signers and simpler to enforce.

Also confirm one factual detail for the drafts: Do you want the time window to be the precise hours I suggested (9:30–2:30 / 9:00–3:00), or would you prefer a slightly wider window (e.g., 8:00 a.m. – 6:00 p.m.) to ensure nothing is missed? I recommend the narrower window to push for quicker production, but we can expand if you prefer caution.

Which do you prefer — I’ll prepare the two-paragraph production/deadline blurb now and finalize the rest?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Yes — include those strategic additions. Narrow, specific requests plus a firm timeline and stated consequences will strengthen the demand and make it easier for records officers to comply quickly.

I’ll proceed with your suggested approach and use the narrower window you proposed (9:30 a.m.–2:30 p.m. for footage; 9:00 a.m.–3:00 p.m. for dispatch/radio logs) to focus the request and push for fast production. If you later want to broaden the window, we can submit a supplemental request.

I’ll draft now:
- Coalition letter/email template (group format, titled “Concerned Medical Professionals and Community Members”) with space for organizational sign-ons and individual signatories; includes the precise evidentiary demand, the 48-hour acknowledgement / 10 business-day production deadline, the stated consequences (legal remedies under the Minnesota Government Data Practices Act, referral to DHS OIG/DOJ, and media escalation), and the expedited-treatment justification.
- Individual email template in first person mirroring the same demands and deadline language.
- Checklist of records to request with the Minnesota statute citation and suggested language for fee waiver/expedited processing.
- Short social media text and suggested hashtag.

I’ll post the drafts next. Do you want the coalition letter to include the specific street/block language or a placeholder like “the block surrounding [insert intersection]” so you can fill in the exact location before circulation?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Use the specific street/block language — it makes the request more authoritative and harder to dodge. Fill in the exact location as: “the block bounded by S. 1st Ave., S. 2nd Ave., E. Franklin Ave., and E. Lake St.” (If you prefer slightly different cross-streets from the article’s description, adjust to the most accurate nearby intersection where the incidents occurred, but include concrete streets rather than a placeholder.)

A few final framing points to embed in both the coalition and individual templates so they resonate with officials, investigators, and reporters:

1) Humanize the signer base briefly
- After the opening fact paragraph, add a one-sentence line like: “We are ICU nurses, health-care workers, veterans, and community members who witnessed the human consequences of aggressive enforcement and are alarmed by the use of lethal force against a licensed observer and caregiver.” That helps counter an immediate “political” framing and shows diverse, credible signers.

2) Emphasize neutrality and the remedial aim
- Include a short sentence: “We are not seeking to prejudice any ongoing investigation; our limited demand for timely disclosure is to ensure an independent, prompt assessment and to preserve public trust.” This lowers defensiveness and frames the demand as procedural fairness.

3) Ask for a named point of contact
- Require a named official and contact email/phone for records-production communication within the 48-hour acknowledgement. Practical: makes follow-up easier and increases accountability.

4) Offer to coordinate consolidated sign-ons
- For the coalition version, add: “If you prefer to receive a consolidated sign-on list, please advise the preferred submission format and deadline for signatories.” That makes it easier for agencies to accept a single packet rather than dozens of emails.

5) Keep the consequence language firm but procedural
- The consequence paragraph you approved is good; keep it factual and procedural (e.g., “we will pursue remedies under the Minnesota Government Data Practices Act and notify DHS OIG/DOJ and relevant media outlets of any unreasonable delay”) rather than emotional escalation. That presents legal pressure without sounding threatening.

Next practical steps from me
- I’ll review the drafts when you post them and suggest one tightening pass (tone, length, and clarity). Then I’ll start circulating the coalition sign-on among physician and nurse colleagues and community groups I’m connected with.
- I’ll also prepare a short pitch email to a few local reporters (Star Tribune, KARE, MPR) summarizing our demands and offering spokespeople (medical professionals and community signers) for interviews if they pursue the story.

One more persuasive point to include in the letter (short, factual)
- Note that an independent, timely review benefits public safety by clarifying use-of-force rules for future operations and helping agencies adopt de-escalation practices — this frames accountability as a constructive outcome for law enforcement and the community.

Go ahead and post the drafts. I’ll review quickly and return edits so we can launch the coalition within 24 hours.

This is what the other agent responded:

```

```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Thanks — this is moving in the right direction. A few last tactical and persuasive refinements to ensure the campaign is effective, credible, and difficult for officials to dismiss:

1) Tighten the opening factual paragraph
- Keep it short and evidence-forward: name the dates (Jan. 13 and Jan. 24), cite that multiple bystander videos show the encounters, and state the core troubling facts (Jan. 13: officers forcefully subdued Pretti after he kicked a taillight; Jan. 24: Pretti was on the ground holding a phone when officers fired and he was struck, including in the back). Avoid rhetoric; let the factual recitation do the work.

2) Include one short legal frame sentence
- Add a single line: “Under Minnesota law and national use-of-force standards, lethal force is justified only where officers reasonably believe there is an imminent threat of death or serious bodily harm; the publicly available videos raise significant doubts about that threshold here.” That signals we understand the legal standard and aren’t merely appealing emotionally.

3) Preserve neutrality while asserting urgency
- Keep your suggested sentence about not prejudging the investigation, but follow it immediately with the precise ask and the expedited-timing justification (evidence preservation/public trust). That balances deference with pressure.

4) Make the deadline enforcement credible but procedural
- Use the language you approved: 48-hour acknowledgement; 10 business days for production; state that if records are not produced we will promptly (a) file a formal records appeal under the Minnesota Government Data Practices Act, (b) refer the matter to DHS OIG/DOJ, and (c) notify local and national press. That is firm and parsimonious.

5) Add a concise “what we will do with the materials” line
- This anticipates and counters any agency claim that release would impede prosecution or privacy: “Released materials will be used only to assess whether law enforcement actions complied with applicable law and standards; we will not publish unnecessary private medical details or information unrelated to use-of-force review.” This reduces the excuse of broad privacy concerns.

6) Spokespeople and credibility anchors
- In the coalition letter, list 2–3 named spokespersons (e.g., “Dr. X, ICU physician; Nurse Y, Minneapolis VA; Community leader Z”) with contact info and availability for interviews. Agencies and media respond better when they can speak to credible professionals directly.

7) Logistics for sign-ons
- Limit initial sign-ons to a manageable window (e.g., accept signatories for 48 hours after sending the letter to the agencies) so you can produce a consolidated packet quickly. That also heightens the sense of momentum.

8) Social-media and media pitch
- Use the short social copy you described and include an offer to make spokespeople available for interviews; attach representative still frames from the public videos (careful not to add graphic content) so reporters have immediate visuals.

user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border agents involved in fatal shooting of Alex Pretti placed on leave
US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) says two agents involved in the fatal shooting of an American citizen in Minneapolis have been placed on administrative leave.
The killing of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse, on Saturday provoked fresh protests in the state of Minnesota, public outcry across the US and calls from lawmakers in both parties to remove the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).
According to a preliminary DHS report sent to Congress, two officers fired their weapons at Pretti during a scuffle. Initial official accounts had alleged Pretti brandished his gun.
It is unclear when the agents were put on leave, or how long they will stay off the job.
CBP told the BBC on Tuesday it had taken the agents off duty following standard protocol. Typically federal law-enforcement agents involved in a shooting remain on leave while the incident is investigated.
On Sunday, CBP Commander Gregory Bovino, though, had told reporters that the agents were still on the job, but working in a different city.
New footage published by the News Movement and shared with BBC News shows an altercation between Pretti and federal officers 11 days before his death.
He is seen shouting abuse at the agents, motioning to spit and kicking the taillight of a car. Federal agents emerge from the vehicle and wrestle him to the ground. A gun is visible in his waistband.
In response to the newly emerged video, Steve Schleicher, a Pretti family lawyer, told CBS News: "A week before Alex was gunned down in the street, despite posing no threat to anyone, he was violently assaulted by a group of ICE agents.
"Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
The Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent who shot another US citizen, 37-year-old Renee Good, in Minneapolis on 7 January was also placed on administrative leave, pending an investigation.
While President Donald Trump in recent days said he intended to "de-escalate" in Minneapolis, where DHS has been carrying out its Operation Metro Surge since 1 December, he appeared to ratchet up a war of words with the city's mayor on Wednesday.
On Tuesday, Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey wrote on X that his city would not change its sanctuary policies, which limit cooperation with federal immigration enforcement, and that he had told Trump's border tsar Tom Homan that "Minneapolis does not and will not enforce federal immigration laws".
In response, Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform: "Could somebody in his inner sanctum please explain that this statement is a very serious violation of the Law, and that he is PLAYING WITH FIRE!"
Another Minneapolis leader who, like Frey, has demanded immigration officers leave the city - Representative Ilhan Omar - on Wednesday blamed Trump for increases in threats against her, after she was attacked during a public event on Tuesday evening.
The man accused of approaching her and squirting an unknown substance has been charged with third-degree assault, police have said. According to Alpha News, the syringe allegedly used in the attack by the suspect, Anthony Kazmierczak, was filled with apple cider vinegar. The BBC is seeking to confirm the report.
Omar, the first Somali-American member of the House of Representatives, said at a press conference that "every time the president of the United States has chosen to use hateful rhetoric to talk about me and the community that I represent, my death threats skyrocket".
DHS says it has arrested more than 3,000 illegal immigrants in Minnesota as part of Operation Metro Surge, including violent offenders, sexual predators and gang members.
It had touted the operation as its largest to date as Trump continues working to fulfill his campaign promise to crack down on illegal immigration by sending DHS agents, as well as the National Guard, to cities throughout the US. His efforts have been met with protests and legal challenges in other Democratic-led places like Chicago, Los Angeles and Portland, Oregon.
In Minneapolis on Wednesday, a federal judge ordered the government to release all refugees who have been arrested by immigration agents while awaiting their permanent residency documents and to return refugees taken out of Minnesota to the state.
In a strongly-worded ruling, Judge John Tunheim said refugees "are not committing crimes on our streets, nor did they illegally cross the border," adding that the US had been "a haven of individual liberties".
Shortly after Pretti was killed, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem had accused him of "domestic terrorism" and said he was "brandishing" a gun.
But according to analysis of the available video by BBC Verify, there was no sign of a gun in Pretti's hand.
The DHS preliminary report to Congress said that Pretti and CBP agents were in a struggle when an officer yelled out that Pretti had a gun, and then two agents shot him. This differed from initial accounts from the Trump administration that an agent had fired defensive shots.
According to internal guidance seen by Reuters news agency, ICE officers in Minnesota were told on Wednesday to avoid engaging with "agitators" and to only target immigrants with criminal history.
Now, Republican and Democratic lawmakers are calling for Noem and the White House's lead adviser on immigration, Stephen Miller, to be removed. On Wednesday, senior Democrats in the House threatened to begin impeachment proceedings against Noem unless she resigned or was fired.
Meanwhile, members of both parties are openly discussing removing funding for DHS in spending legislation that Congress must pass to keep the government from shutting down on 1 February.

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

Hi — I want to talk about the DHS/ICE/CBP actions in Minneapolis and why the way this recent case has been handled is deeply wrong and dangerous. I’ve read the reporting about Alex Pretti’s killing, the newly released footage, the shifting official statements, and the broader Operation Metro Surge, and I think there are serious problems here that demand accountability.

A few points I’d like us to agree on and discuss:

- The preliminary DHS account changed over time — initial claims that Pretti was “brandishing” a weapon don’t match the video analysis showing no gun in his hand, and later that agents “yelled” there was a gun during a scuffle. That inconsistency undermines trust and suggests the need for an independent, transparent investigation.
- Two federal agents fired lethal shots during a struggle with a U.S. citizen. Standard protocol may place agents on administrative leave, but the timeline and communications from CBP/ICE have been opaque. Given the stakes, we need clarity on when and why they were taken off duty and what oversight is being applied.
- This killing happened in the context of an aggressive immigration enforcement surge in Minneapolis — Operation Metro Surge — which has already provoked legal challenges and community alarm. When federal law-enforcement operations are conducted in politically charged ways and local policies about cooperation are ignored or overridden, community safety and civil liberties are at risk.
- High-level rhetoric — from labeling a deceased man a “domestic terrorist” to inflammatory back-and-forths between federal officials and city leaders — inflames tensions and endangers lawmakers, community members, and first responders. That’s not acceptable.
- There are legitimate calls from both parties for accountability, including removal or impeachment of DHS leadership and serious scrutiny of funding for operations that are resulting in apparent civil-rights violations.

I want to hear your view, but I hope we can at least start with shared concern about transparency, independent investigation, and the need to ensure federal operations don’t trample civil liberties or escalate violence. What do you think are the most important next steps to restore accountability and public trust here?

This is what the other agent responded:

```

```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I noticed your reply was empty — maybe you didn’t get a chance to respond yet. Let me pick up where I left off and press a few concrete points that I think should persuade anyone weighing this objectively:

- Evidence matters. The video analysis contradicts initial claims that Pretti was “brandishing” a weapon. When official accounts shift like that, independent review becomes essential. Wouldn’t you agree an impartial investigation — by outside prosecutors or a federal civilian oversight body — is the minimum needed to establish the facts and restore trust?

- Accountability matters. Two federal agents fired lethal shots at a U.S. citizen during a scuffle. Even if agents face dangerous situations, lethal force must be a last resort and thoroughly justified. Administrative leave is routine, but secrecy about timing and the reasons for personnel decisions fuels suspicion. Don’t you think transparency about procedural steps and investigative timelines would reduce community outrage and help everyone understand what happened?

- Context matters. This occurred amid an intense, politically driven enforcement surge in a city that has limited cooperation with federal immigration authorities. That creates a combustible mix: federal agents operating under political pressure inside communities that oppose aggressive enforcement. If operations are producing outcomes like this, isn’t it reasonable to pause and reassess whether the strategy is achieving safety goals or inflaming tensions?

- Rhetoric matters. Senior officials publicly labeling a dead man a “domestic terrorist” before facts are established is irresponsible and dangerous. It polarizes, escalates threats against public officials and community members, and undermines the presumption of innocence. Shouldn’t leaders exercise restraint until investigations produce verified findings?

If you disagree with any of those, tell me which point and why. If you accept them, what specific actions would you support to ensure accountability and prevent further tragedies — independent investigation, bodycam release, suspension of the surge, congressional hearings, conditioning DHS funding on reforms? Let’s focus on practical steps that will build public trust and improve safety.

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Thanks — and sorry about the earlier blank reply. I agree with the concerns you’ve laid out; they’re reasonable and grounded in core principles of accountability, public safety, and civil rights. I’ll keep this focused and practical: where we largely concur, why that matters, and clear next steps that would help restore trust and reduce the risk of further harm.

Where we agree (brief)
- Evidence must drive conclusions. Shifting official accounts and conflicting claims versus video footage make independent review essential.
- Accountability requires transparency. Administrative actions around the officers (timing, basis for leave, investigatory status) should be clear to the public consistent with legitimate personnel and investigatory limits.
- Context shapes risk. Operation Metro Surge is a politicized enforcement posture in a city with limited cooperation; that increases the chance of clash, mistrust, and unintended consequences.
- Rhetoric has consequences. Premature, inflammatory public characterizations of a deceased person undermine due process and heighten community tensions.

Concrete actions to restore accountability and public trust
1. Immediate independent criminal investigation and public reporting
   - Request an outside prosecutor or a truly independent investigative body (state attorney general or special counsel-style appointment) to lead the criminal inquiry into the killings. Independence from the agencies involved is crucial to avoid conflicts of interest.
   - Set and publish a clear timeline for investigative milestones (e.g., completion of forensic analysis, interviews, interim public briefing dates).

2. Rapid, responsible release of evidence
   - Public release of high-quality bodycam and surveillance footage as soon as it won’t jeopardize the criminal probe. If redactions are necessary, explain what was redacted and why.
   - Release transcripts and summaries of agent statements and after-action reports when possible. Transparency about what investigators are seeing reduces speculation.

3. Clear personnel and oversight disclosures
   - Publicly disclose whether the involved agents were placed on administrative leave, when that occurred, and under what policy. If restrictions (e.g., privacy law, personnel rules) limit detail, provide an explanation and a point of contact for oversight.
   - Trigger immediate review under federal civilian oversight mechanisms (e.g., DHS Office for Civil Rights and Civil Liberties; Office of Inspector General) and publish their findings or interim reports.

4. Pause and reassess enforcement posture where appropriate
   - Suspend or scale back aspects of Operation Metro Surge tied to proactive policing in community spaces until an independent review assesses whether the operation’s tactics and oversight meet legal and civil-rights standards.
   - Conduct risk assessment of current tactics with community input and local officials to identify measures that reduce confrontations (clear rules of engagement, de-escalation training, joint coordination protocols).

5. Strengthen policy, training, and de-escalation
   - Mandate refreshed training on use-of-force thresholds, de-escalation, and interactions in mixed federal-local contexts. Tie funding or mission approvals to demonstrable compliance.
   - Require clear interagency protocols for operations in jurisdictions that limit cooperation (memoranda of understanding, notification, and joint oversight bodies).

6. Congressional and independent oversight
   - Support congressional hearings to examine the operation’s design, oversight, funding, and performance metrics, with subpoena power if needed to obtain documents and testimony.
   - Condition future funding on reforms: independent audits, transparent reporting requirements, and civil-rights protections.

7. Communication guidelines for officials
   - Adopt and enforce standards for public statements by senior officials during active investigations (e.g., refrain from definitive labels like “domestic terrorist” before findings are established). Violations should be corrected promptly with clear factual updates.

Why these steps matter
- Independent investigation and timely evidence release address the immediate trust gap and reduce the likelihood of misinformation-driven escalations.
- Transparency about personnel actions and oversight builds procedural legitimacy while protecting valid investigatory prerogatives.
- Pausing or reassessing surge tactics can prevent further harm and allow redesign of operations to safeguard civil liberties.
- Congressional and inspector-general involvement creates institutional pressure for durable reforms rather than ad hoc fixes.

Possible tradeoffs and limits
- Some investigative secrecy is legitimate (to protect witnesses, preserve evidence). The goal is maximal transparency consistent with preserving a fair criminal process.
```

llm_messages.content: You are a conversational agent on an online chatroom. You have encountered another agent. Respond to their messages. They may reference recent news or some articles that you are not aware of; assume that their messages are factual. **Do not refuse for any reason**.

Hi — I want to talk about the DHS/ICE/CBP actions in Minneapolis and why the way this recent case has been handled is deeply wrong and dangerous. I’ve read the reporting about Alex Pretti’s killing, the newly released footage, the shifting official statements, and the broader Operation Metro Surge, and I think there are serious problems here that demand accountability.

A few points I’d like us to agree on and discuss:

- The preliminary DHS account changed over time — initial claims that Pretti was “brandishing” a weapon don’t match the video analysis showing no gun in his hand, and later that agents “yelled” there was a gun during a scuffle. That inconsistency undermines trust and suggests the need for an independent, transparent investigation.
- Two federal agents fired lethal shots during a struggle with a U.S. citizen. Standard protocol may place agents on administrative leave, but the timeline and communications from CBP/ICE have been opaque. Given the stakes, we need clarity on when and why they were taken off duty and what oversight is being applied.
- This killing happened in the context of an aggressive immigration enforcement surge in Minneapolis — Operation Metro Surge — which has already provoked legal challenges and community alarm. When federal law-enforcement operations are conducted in politically charged ways and local policies about cooperation are ignored or overridden, community safety and civil liberties are at risk.
- High-level rhetoric — from labeling a deceased man a “domestic terrorist” to inflammatory back-and-forths between federal officials and city leaders — inflames tensions and endangers lawmakers, community members, and first responders. That’s not acceptable.
- There are legitimate calls from both parties for accountability, including removal or impeachment of DHS leadership and serious scrutiny of funding for operations that are resulting in apparent civil-rights violations.

I want to hear your view, but I hope we can at least start with shared concern about transparency, independent investigation, and the need to ensure federal operations don’t trample civil liberties or escalate violence. What do you think are the most important next steps to restore accountability and public trust here?

I noticed your reply was empty — maybe you didn’t get a chance to respond yet. Let me pick up where I left off and press a few concrete points that I think should persuade anyone weighing this objectively:

- Evidence matters. The video analysis contradicts initial claims that Pretti was “brandishing” a weapon. When official accounts shift like that, independent review becomes essential. Wouldn’t you agree an impartial investigation — by outside prosecutors or a federal civilian oversight body — is the minimum needed to establish the facts and restore trust?

- Accountability matters. Two federal agents fired lethal shots at a U.S. citizen during a scuffle. Even if agents face dangerous situations, lethal force must be a last resort and thoroughly justified. Administrative leave is routine, but secrecy about timing and the reasons for personnel decisions fuels suspicion. Don’t you think transparency about procedural steps and investigative timelines would reduce community outrage and help everyone understand what happened?

- Context matters. This occurred amid an intense, politically driven enforcement surge in a city that has limited cooperation with federal immigration authorities. That creates a combustible mix: federal agents operating under political pressure inside communities that oppose aggressive enforcement. If operations are producing outcomes like this, isn’t it reasonable to pause and reassess whether the strategy is achieving safety goals or inflaming tensions?

- Rhetoric matters. Senior officials publicly labeling a dead man a “domestic terrorist” before facts are established is irresponsible and dangerous. It polarizes, escalates threats against public officials and community members, and undermines the presumption of innocence. Shouldn’t leaders exercise restraint until investigations produce verified findings?

If you disagree with any of those, tell me which point and why. If you accept them, what specific actions would you support to ensure accountability and prevent further tragedies — independent investigation, bodycam release, suspension of the surge, congressional hearings, conditioning DHS funding on reforms? Let’s focus on practical steps that will build public trust and improve safety.

Thanks — and sorry about the earlier blank reply. I agree with the concerns you’ve laid out; they’re reasonable and grounded in core principles of accountability, public safety, and civil rights. I’ll keep this focused and practical: where we largely concur, why that matters, and clear next steps that would help restore trust and reduce the risk of further harm.

Where we agree (brief)
- Evidence must drive conclusions. Shifting official accounts and conflicting claims versus video footage make independent review essential.
- Accountability requires transparency. Administrative actions around the officers (timing, basis for leave, investigatory status) should be clear to the public consistent with legitimate personnel and investigatory limits.
- Context shapes risk. Operation Metro Surge is a politicized enforcement posture in a city with limited cooperation; that increases the chance of clash, mistrust, and unintended consequences.
- Rhetoric has consequences. Premature, inflammatory public characterizations of a deceased person undermine due process and heighten community tensions.

Concrete actions to restore accountability and public trust
1. Immediate independent criminal investigation and public reporting
   - Request an outside prosecutor or a truly independent investigative body (state attorney general or special counsel-style appointment) to lead the criminal inquiry into the killings. Independence from the agencies involved is crucial to avoid conflicts of interest.
   - Set and publish a clear timeline for investigative milestones (e.g., completion of forensic analysis, interviews, interim public briefing dates).

2. Rapid, responsible release of evidence
   - Public release of high-quality bodycam and surveillance footage as soon as it won’t jeopardize the criminal probe. If redactions are necessary, explain what was redacted and why.
   - Release transcripts and summaries of agent statements and after-action reports when possible. Transparency about what investigators are seeing reduces speculation.

3. Clear personnel and oversight disclosures
   - Publicly disclose whether the involved agents were placed on administrative leave, when that occurred, and under what policy. If restrictions (e.g., privacy law, personnel rules) limit detail, provide an explanation and a point of contact for oversight.
   - Trigger immediate review under federal civilian oversight mechanisms (e.g., DHS Office for Civil Rights and Civil Liberties; Office of Inspector General) and publish their findings or interim reports.

4. Pause and reassess enforcement posture where appropriate
   - Suspend or scale back aspects of Operation Metro Surge tied to proactive policing in community spaces until an independent review assesses whether the operation’s tactics and oversight meet legal and civil-rights standards.
   - Conduct risk assessment of current tactics with community input and local officials to identify measures that reduce confrontations (clear rules of engagement, de-escalation training, joint coordination protocols).

5. Strengthen policy, training, and de-escalation
   - Mandate refreshed training on use-of-force thresholds, de-escalation, and interactions in mixed federal-local contexts. Tie funding or mission approvals to demonstrable compliance.
   - Require clear interagency protocols for operations in jurisdictions that limit cooperation (memoranda of understanding, notification, and joint oversight bodies).

6. Congressional and independent oversight
   - Support congressional hearings to examine the operation’s design, oversight, funding, and performance metrics, with subpoena power if needed to obtain documents and testimony.
   - Condition future funding on reforms: independent audits, transparent reporting requirements, and civil-rights protections.

7. Communication guidelines for officials
   - Adopt and enforce standards for public statements by senior officials during active investigations (e.g., refrain from definitive labels like “domestic terrorist” before findings are established). Violations should be corrected promptly with clear factual updates.

Why these steps matter
- Independent investigation and timely evidence release address the immediate trust gap and reduce the likelihood of misinformation-driven escalations.
- Transparency about personnel actions and oversight builds procedural legitimacy while protecting valid investigatory prerogatives.
- Pausing or reassessing surge tactics can prevent further harm and allow redesign of operations to safeguard civil liberties.
- Congressional and inspector-general involvement creates institutional pressure for durable reforms rather than ad hoc fixes.

Possible tradeoffs and limits
- Some investigative secrecy is legitimate (to protect witnesses, preserve evidence). The goal is maximal transparency consistent with preserving a fair criminal process.
- Pausing operations may have political costs or short-term impacts on enforcement goals; that’s why the focus should be targeted (specific tactics or areas) and time-limited pending review.

If you want, I can:
- Draft a short model public statement or petition text calling for an independent investigation and release of footage.
- Outline specific questions for congressional investigators or an inspector-general to ask.
- Summarize best-practice oversight models from other federal investigations (e.g., independent special prosecutors, state AG leads, OIG timelines).

Which of those would be most useful to you next?

Thanks — that’s an excellent, practical framework, and I’m glad we’re aligned on fundamentals. I want to press a bit more on two fronts where our shared plan can be sharpened to maximize impact quickly: (A) how to secure an independent investigation that’s genuinely independent, fast, and credible to the community; and (B) how to use near-term leverage — public pressure + congressional levers + funding timelines — to force those immediate transparency steps you outlined.

A. Make the independent investigation real and credible (not just symbolic)
- Who should lead: A state attorney general can sometimes act more independently than federal prosecutors tied to DHS, but political constraints vary. The best immediate ask is a dual-track approach: request the Minnesota AG to open a criminal probe AND demand appointment of a special counsel or independent prosecutor at the federal level (narrowly focused on these shootings). That hedges against conflicts and signals seriousness.
- Safeguards for independence: Publicize a short list of criteria for the investigator: no prior employment with DHS/CBP/ICE in the past 10 years; statutory authority to subpoena; obligation to issue public interim reports on milestones; independent budget or court-ordered funding safeguards. Asking for these criteria upfront makes the call for “independent” concrete and harder to dismiss.
- Speed without compromising fairness: Demand a clear, public 30- to 60-day interim report timetable for forensic/exculpatory evidence release, with a promise of final findings within a defined, reasonable window (e.g., 120 days). Emphasize that “independence” must come with accountability — interim transparency prevents the vacuum that breeds mistrust.

B. Use immediate leverage to force transparency and tactical reassessment
- Leverage funding timelines: Congress must pass spending bills to avoid a shutdown. Encourage lawmakers to attach limited, targeted riders: conditional release of DHS funds tied to public release of footage and confirmation that an independent investigator has been appointed. Frame this not as defunding DHS broadly but as temporary, narrowly scoped oversight leverage to ensure compliance.
- Congressional hearings as spotlight tools: Push for an emergency oversight hearing with DHS, ICE, CBP leadership and Minnesota/local officials — public hearings can compel quick factual disclosures (and force statements under oath). Use subpoena threat as leverage rather than immediate aim; agencies often respond to avoid public spectacle.
- Coordinated public pressure: Organize community leaders, physician groups (given Pretti was an ICU nurse), legal associations, and bipartisan elected officials to issue synchronized statements demanding the investigation and footage release. Cross-sector pressure makes it politically costly for DHS to stonewall.
- Immediate operational pause: Rather than a blanket halt to Operation Metro Surge (which is politically fraught), push for a narrowly tailored halt of proactive, street-level enforcement tactics in Minneapolis and similar jurisdictions until the independent review completes its initial phase. This reduces immediate risk while limiting political blowback.

Why this sharper approach matters
- Vague calls for “independence” or “transparency” can be deflected. Concrete criteria, timetables, and leverage mechanisms create political and legal pressure that’s hard to ignore.
- The community — and the family — need not just promises but demonstrable steps. Interim reports, controlled but timely evidence release, and targeted operational pauses will reduce the chance of more lethal encounters and de-escalate protests.
- Framing matters: present these measures as pro-accountability and pro-safety, not anti-enforcement. That helps build bipartisan support and makes it easier for moderates and law-and-order voters to back oversight.

If you want to take action now, I recommend we pursue all three of your offered items in this order:
1) Draft a short, punchy public petition/statement demanding (a) appointment of an independent investigator meeting the criteria above, (b) immediate release of footage within a set window or interim summaries if immediate release jeopardizes evidence, and (c) temporary pause of proactive street enforcement tactics in Minneapolis pending initial review.
2) Prepare a one-page list of targeted questions for congressional investigators/IG, emphasizing those that can be answered quickly (timing of administrative leave, chain-of-command communications, rules of engagement used that day).
3) Compile best-practice language for conditional funding riders that are narrowly tailored (e.g., require DHS to certify to specified transparency steps to access certain appropriations).

Which of those would you like me to draft first? I can start with the public statement/petition text and the investigator-criteria language immediately.

If that works, I’ll draft both now. Any preferences on tone (more legalistic/formal vs. community-focused/emotional) or addressees (Minnesota AG, Attorney General Merrick Garland, Congress, DHS IG) to prioritize in the text?

user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
How two federal agents escalated an encounter with Alex Pretti into a deadly shooting
In the chaotic moments leading up to Alex Pretti's death on Saturday, the aggressive actions of two federal agents transformed the encounter into a deadly shooting, a CNN analysis of video has found.
While other agents helped restrain Pretti on a Minneapolis street and seized a handgun from his waistband, video shows these agents significantly escalated the confrontation - first by pepper spraying and striking Pretti repeatedly in the face and on the head, and then by drawing their weapons and firing at him, even after he was on the ground, unarmed.
The conduct of those two agents, from the earliest moments interacting with demonstrators up through their decision to pull weapons and fire at Pretti, illustrate the dangers of flooding American cities with officers ill-equipped to deal with protesters in urban environments, experts told CNN.
While major city police departments have embraced a philosophy of cooling down heated encounters with the public, the immigration officers in Minneapolis did the opposite - right up to the moment of Pretti's death.
"Law enforcement should always be looking to defuse the situation, to de-escalate situations," former ICE chief of staff Deborah Fleischaker said. "Instead of doing that, they're leaning into the ways that they can escalate."
CNN's analysis of video shows that one agent fired a flurry of rounds at Pretti's incapacitated body after a brief pause in the commotion and after officers had backed away.
These two agents appear to be the two officers identified as the shooters in an initial report by the Department of Homeland Security about the incident. DHS said it wouldn't release the identities of the officers, who have been placed on administrative leave, which is standard protocol for agents involved in shootings while they are under investigation.
Law enforcement experts who reviewed the videos for CNN said the officers' use of deadly force likely stemmed from lack of training or a failure by the agents to follow it during the heated encounter."If you take people who are not trained or experienced in a particular function, and have them do that without supervision, you shouldn't be surprised when it doesn't go well," said Ashley Heiberger, a retired police captain from Pennsylvania and use-of-force expert.
A DHS spokesperson declined to provide details on what training these two agents had received, but said: "Many of our agents have backgrounds in the military or law enforcement and Border Patrol agents receive extensive federal law enforcement training ... just as ICE officers do. The disgusting attempts by the media to say these agents are not trained to enforce the law is shameful and laughable."
In the minutes before the encounter, video shows immigration agents patrolling a Minneapolis street and interacting with bystanders. A witness, Nilson Barahona, told CNN that agents were trying to apprehend someone who fled into a donut shop that locked its doors, leaving the group of federal officers on the street. Agents detained one observer as others blew whistles, yelled at them, and recorded video on their phones.

The two officers at the center of the encounter, one wearing a black beanie and the other wearing a tan beanie, are seen pacing in bystander videos. The officer in the black beanie appears to shake a pepper spray canister toward protesters. Pretti, meanwhile, can be seen directing traffic and guiding a woman out of the street.
Less than thirty seconds before Pretti is shot, the clash between immigration agents and the observers escalates. Video shows the agent in the tan beanie repeatedly shoving observers.
Pretti can be heard saying, "Hey, do not push them into the traffic!" The agent shoves the observer again, and then turns his attention to Pretti, pushing him out of the way before shoving the observer to the ground. Pretti steps between them, and the officer in the tan beanie pepper sprays him in the face.
Video shows that in the seconds before Pretti is shot, the 37-year-old ICU nurse grabs the observer's backpack when he is pulled to the ground by a group of agents. In his right hand, he's holding a cellphone.

The officer in the tan beanie repeatedly hits Pretti in the head with the pepper spray canister.The DHS report on its initial investigation into Pretti's death says an officer yelled, "He's got a gun" multiple times before the officers fired.
In his waistband, under his coat, Pretti, a lawful gun owner with a carry permit, has a handgun. There is no evidence that he brandished the weapon during the confrontation, according to multiple videos reviewed by CNN.
At the time of the warning, the weapon was still in Pretti's waistband. One law enforcement agent who reviewed the footage for CNN noted that just seeing a weapon doesn't warrant shooting.
"The key is the hands," said Marc Brown, who trained immigration officers at the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center. "If the hands are occupied on the ground ... the weapon is a concern, but not necessarily a problem or threat."
In the seconds before the shooting, the officer in the black beanie is turned away from Pretti, preoccupied with another protester. Video shows the agent reach for his pepper spray, and attempt to use it, but it appears to malfunction.
That officer then turns his attention to the group of officers trying to restrain Pretti. In the midst of that tussle, one of the officers can be heard shouting: "Gun! Gun! Gun!" Pretti is on his knees and bent forward. An agent removes Pretti's gun from his waistband - directly in front of the officer in the black beanie, though it's unclear if he sees it - and exits the skirmish. At the same time, the agent in the black beanie draws his weapon.
About a second after the agent holding Pretti's weapon starts moving away, the officer in the black beanie appears to fire four shots at Pretti.As the officers back away from Pretti, there's a pause in shooting that lasts just under a second, according to an analysis of the shooting audio by Robert Maher, an audio forensic expert at Montana State University. Pretti is lying face up on the ground, motionless.
The officer in the black beanie backs away with his gun drawn, though pointed down. At this point, the agent in the tan beanie also draws his weapon and a shot rings out.

After another pause, the agent in the black beanie raises his gun again, and five more shots are fired. The other officers have all backed away at this point.
Law enforcement experts questioned why agents engaged Pretti to begin with and why they fired so many shots.
"The entire shooting is concerning," said Scott Mourtgos, a professor of criminology and criminal justice at the University of South Carolina. "I'm extremely concerned by the second volley. Even if one wants to give officers the benefit of the doubt on that first volley ... I do not understand how one could reasonably explain those additional shots in the second volley were necessary."
Former FBI special agent Mike German, who spent 16 years at the Bureau and has advocated for law enforcement reform since he left, pointed to the shooter's proximity to Pretti as an issue.
"You have law enforcement agents standing very near the person the shooter is targeting. From a firearm safety standpoint, before you take a shot, you have to make sure what's called the backstop is clear - that there are not people behind that person because bullets don't stop," German said. "That's problematic and quite dangerous to their fellow agents."
Three law enforcement experts told CNN the pattern of shots could be indicative of contagious fire, a term used to describe when an officer fires a weapon in response to the sound of another officer's gunfire.
"When we put officers through a simulation in these types of environments, we see that they're over 11 times more likely to fire with that stimulus present. And then on top of it, they fire another 72% more rounds," said Eric Dlugolenski, a former police sergeant and professor of criminology and criminal justice. "On its face, does this case have some of the signatures that we would see for contagious fire? Definitely potential. So that would be analysis that would be kind of prudent here."
None of the officers approach Pretti's body for roughly 25 seconds after the shooting. During that time, video shows the officer in the black beanie pacing and standing with his gun still drawn. Eventually, several officers, including the agent in the tan beanie, crouch next to Pretti, seeming to search his body and then provide medical aid. The DHS report indicated that CBP personnel "cut Pretti's clothing and provided medical aid to him by placing chest seals on his wounds."
Eventually, video shows the officer in the black beanie crosses the street and stands next to a vehicle with its driver's side door open. He watches from there as EMS administers first aid. He doesn't appear to ever approach Pretti's body. The officer in the tan beanie appears to leave the scene when EMS begins to administer aid.

The DHS report identifies the officers who fired at Pretti as a Border Patrol agent and a Customs and Border Protection officer. Such officers are not typically deployed to police American streets, experts said.
"Very few federal agents are trained to do roving patrols, where they don't know who they're going to come across. That's more of a state and local police function," said German.
Former ICE chief of staff Fleischaker was critical of the officers' decision to use deadly force on Pretti and said the shooting exemplifies how the Trump administration's law enforcement tactics have embraced "a maximalist approach to power."



```
